title: Benchmark Datasets for Novel View Synthesis
content:
## The Four Standard Benchmarks

The 3DGS compression literature evaluates almost exclusively on four datasets. Knowing their characteristics is essential for understanding which methods generalize and which overfit to specific scene types.

## Tanks and Temples

**Type**: Large-scale outdoor and indoor capture with industrial equipment
**Scenes**: Truck, Train, Playground, M60, and others
**Properties**:
- High-resolution captures, real-world lighting
- Unbounded scenes with complex backgrounds
- Objects of varying scales (a full tank vs. a train car)
- Challenging for 3DGS because the background extends to infinity

**Why it matters**: The Truck scene is the de facto single-scene benchmark. Most papers report per-scene numbers here, and the Truck has become a calibration point: you can gauge a paper's claim by comparing its Truck PSNR to baseline 3DGS.

## Deep Blending

**Type**: Indoor scenes captured with depth sensors
**Scenes**: Indoor room scans (DrJohnson, Playroom)
**Properties**:
- Contains challenging near-field geometry
- Significant depth of field and occlusion
- Scenes with specular surfaces (windows, floors)

**Why it matters**: Tests view-dependent color representation (SH) quality. Methods that aggressively reduce SH degree often show the largest PSNR drops on Deep Blending.

## Mip-NeRF 360

**Type**: 360° capture of unbounded indoor and outdoor scenes
**Scenes**: garden, bicycle, bonsai, counter, kitchen, room, staircase
**Properties**:
- Cameras placed inside the scene, pointing outward — the "unbounded" challenge
- Background extends to infinity in all directions
- Wide variation in scene complexity
- 9 scenes, making it statistically robust

**Why it matters**: The unbounded nature requires either spatial contraction (MipNeRF360's \(\text{contract}\) function) or large numbers of Gaussians to cover the background. Compression methods that use spatial quantization must handle the highly non-uniform spatial distribution of Gaussians.

## Synthetic NeRF (Blender)

**Type**: Computer-generated scenes with known ground truth geometry
**Scenes**: chair, drums, ficus, hotdog, lego, materials, mic, ship
**Properties**:
- Perfect ground-truth images (no camera noise, no motion blur)
- Objects on white backgrounds with complex materials
- PSNR values are systematically higher (~28–35 dB) than real captures

**Why it matters**: The clean ground truth makes this ideal for measuring rendering quality without confounding factors. Results here are not directly comparable to real-capture datasets — PSNR values will be higher and you should not compare them directly.

## Cross-Dataset Generalization

A key warning from the 3DGS.zip survey: **methods optimized on one dataset may not generalize**. The Synthetic NeRF dataset's object-centric, bounded setup is very different from Mip-NeRF 360's unbounded 360° captures. A compression method that aggressively prunes Gaussians based on viewing-frequency statistics tuned to bounded scenes may badly over-prune the infinite background in unbounded scenes.

references.label: Knapitsch et al. 2017
references.title: Tanks and Temples: Benchmarking Large-Scale Scene Reconstruction

references.label: Barron et al. 2022
references.title: Mip-NeRF 360: Unbounded Anti-Aliased Neural Radiance Fields
